items.q: What does in-order traversal of a binary search tree produce?
items.misconception: Nodes in the order they were inserted.
items.why: Students forget the BST property: left subtree less than node less than right subtree. In-order (left, node, right) therefore visits values in sorted order - regardless of insertion order.

items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.

items.q: Why are passwords stored as hashes rather than plaintext?
items.correct: Hashes are one-way - even an attacker who steals them cannot easily recover passwords.
items.misconception: Hashes are encryption that can be decrypted with the right key.
items.random_wrong: Hashes compress the data to save space.
items.why: Students confuse hashing with encryption. Hashes have NO reverse function - you can verify a password by re-hashing, but cannot decrypt the stored hash to get the original.
The answer examiners want is: same input -> same output. “always different output” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's ODEs/PDEs. “event lists” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Halving time step usually means error.
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
